quote: I went pretty far with one company, and we got to the end. And, they they weren't completely being open and giving me all the information because at that time, Santee Cooper was giving a $9,000 rebate.
stewart_read: Elliot is not just sharing a preference — he's disclosing a specific betrayal that stopped a prior deal. This is a trust gift: he's telling Holland exactly what disqualified the last company and implicitly asking whether Ion is different. Holland's response ('Honesty is definitely one of the things you should look for') is empathetic in tone but generic — it validates the principle without differentiating Ion or making any specific commitment about how Ion handles disclosures. The strategic read would be that Holland is saving the differentiation pitch for the closer; the reactive read is that she missed the moment to anchor Ion's identity against Elliot's stated criterion. Given that Holland never returns to this theme, the reactive read holds.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland is deliberately deferring the Ion-differentiation pitch to the closer, keeping setter scope clean.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play this clip and ask Holland: 'What did Elliot just tell you about what will make or break this deal?' The answer is transparency. Then ask: 'What did you say that told him Ion is different?' The gap is the missing specific bridge — something like: 'That's exactly why our process works the way it does — our specialist walks through every number with you on the call so nothing is hidden.' No fabricated figures needed; the intent is the move.

quote: I'd say the average might be $2.25 to $2.50, somewhere in that range.
stewart_read: Elliot provides his average monthly bill unprompted and with reasonable precision. Holland's response — 'We can definitely help get that down quite a bit' — is the textbook 'ask + acknowledge + move on' failure pattern the codex flags as universal across the corpus. The bill is captured as data and immediately dropped. No emotional flip, no alignment-against-the-utility, no curiosity created around what that number means over time. The bill anchor is the highest-leverage moment in any setter call and it was treated as a checkbox.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland is keeping the bill conversation brief to avoid scope creep, leaving the financial detail for the closer.
coaching_implication: This is the #1 codex-flagged universal gap. In the 1-on-1, ask Holland: 'After Elliot told you his bill, what did you do with that number?' Then show the codex's capture-acknowledge-flip pattern. The flip doesn't require invented savings figures — the move is emotional: 'That's real money every single month going to Santee Cooper. That's what we're trying to change.' The pain the number represents is the leverage, not the number itself.

quote: I have an electric car. So that would be, something that I'm and it's a Tesla, so I like the brand.
stewart_read: Elliot volunteers two compounding enthusiasm signals in one breath: he owns a Tesla EV (meaningfully higher electric load, direct brand affinity with the Powerwall) and he's already thinking about battery storage. Holland acknowledges the brand affinity ('Tesla's a good brand') but does not anchor on the EV as a load driver or use it to sharpen the battery conversation. The EV is the single best reason Elliot's bill is what it is and the single best reason the Powerwall conversation is especially relevant for him — neither connection is made.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland is correctly deferring the EV/battery detail to the closer, keeping setter scope clean.
coaching_implication: The strategic read (defer to closer) is partially correct — the full battery conversation belongs with the specialist. But the enthusiasm signal should be anchored, not just acknowledged. Suggested bridge: 'With an EV charging at home, the battery conversation is especially relevant for you — that's exactly what our specialist is built to walk through.' This takes 10 seconds, creates curiosity, and ties the appointment to something Elliot already cares about. Play this clip and ask Holland: 'What did Elliot just tell you that makes this appointment more valuable than a standard solar call?'

quote: I just am not too sure what they are, and I don't wanna accidentally mislead you with any information.
stewart_read: Holland's admission that she doesn't know the full battery lineup is honest and trust-building — given Elliot's stated criterion of transparency (02:27), this is actually the right move and functions as a second data point that Ion operates differently from the company that burned him. The honesty is correct. The only gap is the missing bridge: Holland does not explicitly connect her transparency to Elliot's story, and does not route the battery question to the closer as the person who can answer it fully. The coaching point is not the honesty — it's the absent bridge.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland's admission lands as a second instance of incomplete information, reinforcing Elliot's prior negative experience.
coaching_implication: Affirm the honesty — it's the right call and it's working. The coaching gap is the missing bridge. After 'I don't want to accidentally mislead you,' the next line should route: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — he'll walk through all the battery options with you on the call tomorrow.' This closes the loop and makes the appointment the answer to Elliot's question rather than leaving it open.

quote: It hasn't been recent that I've got into it. I keep on getting ads on the Internet for four different places. That's why I'm talking to you now.
stewart_read: Elliot explicitly discloses he is in multiple funnels simultaneously — 'four different places.' This is the verify.prior_designs signal and it was not followed up. Holland does not ask whether Elliot has seen a design, received a quote, or is actively comparing companies. This is a competitive-context gap: the closer walks into the appointment without knowing whether Elliot has a competing proposal in hand, which materially affects how the closer should frame Ion's offer.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland judges the competitive context as low-risk given Elliot's cooperative tone and moves on to protect call momentum.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, flag this as a handoff gap rather than a lost-deal risk. The question is simple: 'Have any of those other companies shown you a design yet?' One question, 10 seconds, and the closer knows whether they're walking into a comparison or a first look. Add this to the handoff brief coaching: what the closer doesn't know can cost the sit.

stewart_read: Holland confirms the wife is on the title at 02:08 and does not include her in the appointment. The appointment is booked for Elliot alone at 09:08. Per protocols.spouse_decision, a co-title-holder must be present at the appointment — this is a protocol requirement, not a persuasion judgment. The appointment is structurally vulnerable: if the wife is not present, the closer cannot close, and the sit may not qualify for setter compensation.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland judges Elliot as the primary decision-maker and books him alone to avoid scheduling friction, planning to note the wife's involvement in the handoff.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-stakes structural gap in the call. In the 1-on-1, walk Holland through the codex rule: the spouse-on-title question is not a rapport question — it's a protocol trigger. The correct move at 02:08 is: 'Since she's on the title, we'd want her on the call too — what time works for both of you?' Play SESSION18_fd078269 (Randy/Juan) as the positive exemplar. Flag this appointment as fragile in the handoff brief.

quote: I'm gonna send you a text from just my personal number. Do you think you can just send me a photo of that bar graph?
stewart_read: Holland routes bill collection through her personal phone number — the exact anti-pattern the codex flags for security, privacy, and scale exposure. The codex notes this pattern explicitly: 'rep gives out personal phone number for bill follow-up' is the detection signal. Elliot cooperates immediately, so there is no customer-facing harm in this instance, but the structural problem is real: personal number exposure, no systematic follow-up if the photo doesn't arrive, and no bill-capture record tied to the lead.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland uses her personal number because Ion has no automated bill-capture system yet — this is a floor-wide workaround, not an individual rep failure.
coaching_implication: This is a floor-level coaching note, not just a Holland note. The alternative hypothesis is correct — this is a systemic gap, not a rep error. Flag for the manager: until Stewart's automated bill-capture ships, this is the workaround in use across the floor. The coaching conversation with Holland is about awareness of the exposure, not blame. If the bill photo doesn't arrive before the appointment, the closer walks in blind on usage data.

quote: Well, I just have a question for you then. Have you been to Hawaii?
stewart_read: The appointment is booked and confirmed at 09:10. Holland then extends the conversation with a personal rapport question that runs for over four minutes (10:43–15:15). The rapport itself is genuine and the relationship quality at call-end is strong — Elliot is warm, engaged, and sharing freely. The sequencing gap is that 4 of 6 required button-up elements were delivered (datetime confirmed, one-hour block not stated, phone appointment specified implicitly, no-driving/computer anchor stated, 'is that fair' handshake absent, text follow-up promised) before the rapport extension began. The missing elements — the 'is that fair' verbal handshake and the explicit one-hour block — are the coaching gap, not the rapport itself.
alternative_hypothesis: The post-booking rapport is pure time waste with no business value.
coaching_implication: Reframe this for Holland as a sequencing issue, not a rapport issue. The rapport is working — Elliot is clearly comfortable and the relationship is strong. The coaching point is: complete all 6 button-up elements before extending socially. Specifically, the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake (codex: 'predictive of show rate, don't skip it') and the one-hour block statement were both missing. In the 1-on-1, ask: 'What would have happened if you'd said — is that fair? — before asking about Hawaii?' The answer is a stronger appointment anchor with the same warm close.
